title: NOJV — Where Is The Time Going?
description: Breakdown of request time across application code (manual instrumentation) vs HTTP server (auto), DB client (pg auto), and Redis client (ioredis auto). PromQL queries may need metric-name adjustment after first verification — check Grafana Explore for actually-exported names. Common variants seen across OTel SDK versions: http.server.request.duration may be exported as http_server_request_duration_seconds OR http_server_duration_milliseconds; db.client.operation.duration similarly varies between *_seconds and *_milliseconds.

options.content:
## Where Is The Time Going?

Compares **manual app-level latency** (`api_request_duration_seconds` — measured around our route handlers) against **auto-instrumented component latencies** (HTTP server, Postgres client, Redis client) so we can attribute total request time to the right layer.

### Caveat — auto-instrumentation metric names vary by OTel SDK version

Open Grafana Explore on the Hosted Prometheus datasource and confirm the actual exported names before trusting these panels:

- HTTP server: try `http_server_request_duration_seconds_bucket` first; fallback `http_server_duration_milliseconds_bucket` (older SDKs).
- Postgres pg client: try `db_client_operation_duration_seconds_bucket`; fallback `db_client_operation_duration_milliseconds_bucket`.
- ioredis client: try `redis_client_duration_seconds_bucket`; fallback `redis_client_command_duration_milliseconds_bucket` or `db_client_operation_duration_*` (some setups label-tag redis under the same db.client metric with `db.system="redis"`).

If a panel shows "No data", edit the panel target — the bucket suffix is the only thing that should change. If unit is milliseconds, divide by 1000 inside the histogram_quantile call.
